sinopse: Project Hail Mary is one of those rare sci-fi films that manages to feel clever, emotional, and genuinely entertaining at the same time. It may not reach the philosophical depth of Arrival or the cosmic grandeur of Interstellar, but it succeeds beautifully at being something warmer and more intimate: a story about survival, communication, friendship, and hope.What works best is the human core of the film. Beneath the science, the mystery, and the space setting, there is a very sincere emotional journey. The story keeps unfolding in a way that stays engaging, and the central relationship ends up becoming the true heart of the film. That is where it really shines.Ryan Gosling delivers a very likable performance, balancing intelligence, vulnerability, and humor without ever losing the emotional weight of the role. The film also deserves credit for making complicated scientific ideas feel accessible and fun without talking down to the audience.Visually, it is strong and immersive, but the real strength of Project Hail Mary is not spectacle alone. It is the sense of wonder and humanity running through the entire experience. The film knows when to be exciting, when to be funny, and when to slow down and let the emotional moments land.If there is one limitation, it is that the film does not aim for the same existential or philosophical impact as the greatest sci-fi classics. It is more heartfelt than profound. But that is not really a weakness so much as its identity.Overall, Project Hail Mary is a smart, moving, and very satisfying sci-fi adventure with real heart. A film that reminds you that science fiction works best not only when it explores the universe, but when it explores connection as well.

The Housemaid is ridiculously fun, so contrived but ever so entertaining. This in part is helped by the go for broke acting of the leads, especially Seyfried. From the get go it is obvious who the real villain of the story is, but it is the why that keeps the intrigue intact until the reveal. This is a very much throwaway popcorn movie that delivers the goods. In the olden days it would be just the actresses giving the T & A, but now the guys get to show a bit of flesh too ... lol. This isn't any prestige movie, but it provides a fun and entertaining time at the movies.

The drama might be one of the most uncomfortable and awkward watches, which is a massive compliment.I went into it thinking the film was going to operate on a "Materialist" level of heartfelt humour. Instead, it surprised me when it took a darker turn, and it worked perfectly.The awkwardness and discomfort are driven by the impressive performances from Zendaya and Patterson, two actors at the top of their game. Patterson's ability to make me physically cringe is unmatched here.What the film succeeds at is balancing its humour between genuine laughs and dark humour. It also impressed me through its direction and editing. The use of flashbacks and almost dreamlike sequences works to blur reality, adding another layer that elevates it above other films of a similar genre.At no point does the film feel stagnant. There are constant moments driven by character decisions that made the audience audibly gasp. It is an uncomfortable and painful ride until the end, and that is a good thing.

Worst movie ever. Completely predictable from start to finish, with zero surprises. The protagonist is an idiot who constantly makes decisions without thinking, making it frustrating to watch. There's no depth to the characters, no meaningful development, and the dialogue feels flat and unrealistic. It tries to be intense and emotional but fails miserably, coming off as forced and shallow.

I guess it was a difficult project to put kenau reeves in this pathetic character who comes across as contrived. The scene with Martin Scorsese was a saving grace for this film. I ask, does fame and fortune put peoples ego on another level of self referential dribble? It may have worked if it was a drama .
This is the 13th film collaboration betweenBen StillerandOwen Wilson.
FromA Minecraft MovieandThe Super Mario Galaxy MovietoFive Nights at Freddy's 2, see how IMDb users ranked these 50+ video game movie adaptations.

The first Super Mario Bros movie hit me so perfectly (9 stars). I wasn't expecting that level with the sequel, but the drop off was still noticeable. I'll try to explain why, but just know that I still had a great time and was smiling for most of the runtime. And I recommend seeing it on ScreenX. More than half the movie uses the side screens.This sequel feels a little empty. The first one is far from deep, but I feel like I had more to latch onto. This one feels like random events taking place. The story feels generally scattered. And I wasn't as invested in the characters.The clearest drop off is the comedy. I laughed about 10 times. The first one had me cracking up regularly, including a few scenes where I was crying from laughter (Peaches song lmao). I would guess it's a combination of weaker writing and fewer attempts.They also seem to be stretching with the references from the different games. I've played like a hundred Mario or Mario-related games. But many of them I haven't played in over a decade. I recognized significantly fewer references than in the first movie.I love Mario. I still want more movies. I just hope they can reach the levels of magic we felt with the first one.(1 viewing, opening Tuesday ScreenX 3/31/2026)

Nepotism production to give Sadie a film,this girl isn't acting material..
As for singing,don't expect a professional career"on your own"with that either.Was a clichéd mess & honestly boring! No laughs,cringe moments & so forth.But if your wanting background noise & a mindless watch,this is perfect for you.Casting to acting,wasn't overall great & cinematography fine..
Any decent actors had cameo parts,think was to bring a bigger audience.Skip this & watch something else,about roommates or college orientated.

Watching The Mummy from a different point of view really makes it stand out. The blend of exorcism-style horror with the classic mummy concept feels fresh and engaging. The performance of the character portraying the ghost is especially impressive and adds a strong eerie presence throughout the film.The movie delivers a proper edge-of-the-seat experience, supported by excellent sound design and cinematography that enhance the tension in every scene. While the ending could have been more logical and intense, the current one still works well enough.Overall, it's a great watch with a unique horror blend and strong performances that keep you hooked till the end.

titulo: The Legend of Aang: The Last Airbender
sinopse: Like her character, Dionne Quan is also blind in real life.

Send Help (2026) is a surprisingly satisfying watch after a long time. The movie keeps things simple and engaging without trying to be overly deep. The survival aspect is handled well enough to keep you hooked, and the ending feels complete and rewarding.There are a few small plot holes here and there, but they don't really ruin the experience. It's not a movie that demands heavy thinking-just a solid, casual watch you can enjoy from start to finish.Overall, not perfect, but definitely worth watching if you just want something interesting and satisfying.

Crime 101 is a solid, well-mounted crime thriller that works in parts but never quite elevates itself into something memorable. The setup is strong - a disciplined lone thief and a persistent detective circling each other - and there are stretches where the writing feels sharp and engaging. The film looks polished, with slick cinematography and a controlled tone that suits the material.However, it becomes increasingly predictable as it progresses. The characters, while interesting on paper, aren't explored deeply enough to create real emotional or psychological impact. The middle portion especially drags, with pacing issues and repetitive beats that dilute the tension.That said, the film redeems itself with a gripping final 20 minutes. The climax is tense, well-executed, and finally delivers on the promise of the premise.Overall, Crime 101 is a decent watch - stylish and occasionally compelling, but ultimately held back by a sagging middle and underdeveloped characters. 6.5/10.

Michael Jackson grows up in a strict household with his brothers, sisters and parents. His father, Joseph Jackson, played convincingly by Colman Domingo, pushes his sons to the limit because he believes they are destined for greatness. Discipline is not optional. Saying no is not an option either. As Michael gets older, he starts to outgrow his father's control and wants to go solo. Breaking free is not easy and the road toward independence is long and rough, especially when it means going directly against the man who built him.I grew up with Michael Jackson's music, like millions around the world. He was not just famous, he was something else entirely. As John Branca once said, there has never been a bigger star and there likely never will be. That makes this role almost impossible to carry. Jaafar Jackson had enormous shoes to fill playing his uncle and you feel that weight throughout the film. At times you are watching the story, but you are also aware of who this is and what it represents. You already know who will benefit most from this project.The story itself covers ground that many people already know. Because of that, it feels more like a film made for a younger audience who did not experience Michael's life in real time, like my daughter. Watching it with her added something, but it also made clear where the film falls short.The biggest issue is the lack of emotional depth. The characters feel flat, especially Michael himself. There are hardly any moments where you truly get inside his head. You never really see what he is thinking or feeling. Important relationships that could have added layers to his character are missing and it shows. This is called Michael, but it often feels more like a broader Jackson family story, focused on him breaking free from his father rather than understanding who he was as a person.That becomes a problem in the more dramatic moments. When the film tries to hit emotional highs, they do not land the way they should. His relationship with his mother stays on the surface, never becoming something you feel. So when the story reaches points that should carry weight, the impact just is not there.The makeup is also inconsistent. Early on, it is very clear you are watching Jaafar and not Michael, which breaks the illusion. As the film progresses, that improves and there are moments where the transformation becomes convincing. For a first acting role, Jaafar deserves credit. Taking on a figure like Michael Jackson, who also happens to be his uncle, is not just a professional challenge but an emotional one. You can sense the effort, and at times it pays off.Still, knowing who Michael Jackson was, a perfectionist who demanded excellence in everything, it is hard not to feel that this film settles for being just good. And for a story like this, good does not quite cut it.For those who lived through his rise, this will likely feel like a missed opportunity. For others, it might work better. Either way, it never fully captures the man it is named after.

Ben Wheatley doesn't make films for comfort-and Normal sticks to that rule. It's strange, unsettling, and intentionally off-balance. The kind of film that doesn't explain itself, and honestly, doesn't care if you keep up.What works is the atmosphere. It's controlled chaos-quiet moments that feel wrong, and sudden shifts that hit without warning. The storytelling isn't linear in the traditional sense, but that's exactly the point. It pulls you in, disorients you, and leaves you figuring things out on your own.But that same strength can push people away. It's not always easy to follow, and at times it feels more experimental than engaging.Final Verdict: Not made for mass appeal. Made for those who like their cinema unpredictable.Cinema wins. Mediocrity loses.

There is something about A24, a certain sense of fatigue. Once an indie production company synonymous with modern arthouse elevated horror, it now often feels like a rabbit hole for ambitious ideas that never fully land.That's exactly where "Undertone," the debut feature from director Ian Tuason, left me.Maybe I should start with a disclaimer: I'm not the right audience for this kind of film-and that matters. "Undertone" plays within a very specific mix of digital obsessive trends-true-crime podcasts, post-lockdown vibes, and the cringe idea of resurfacing demonic urban legends-without ever transcending them.If creepypasta-fueled supernatural horror is haunting your late-night guilty pleasures, this film is definitely for you. But for someone like me, who gets haunted by the most unsettling and disturbing stories-the lingering ones, the ones I can relate to, feel, or think about for months or years-this is an easy skip, or at least something to approach with very low expectations."Undertone" is an experiment in atmosphere. Evy (Nina Kiri, carrying the weight of the film almost entirely on her own) spends the whole runtime inside her house, caring for her dying mother, who lies in a catatonic state.Her only escape is the podcast Undertone, which she runs with Justin (Adam DiMarco), whose ambiguous role becomes one of the film's few points of intrigue-or at least the only element that gave me some genuinely chilling doubts. But no spoilers here, and intrigue alone isn't enough.When Justin proposes an episode about ten disturbing audio tapes sent to him by a mysterious follower, Evy feels an immediate pull-something she needs to dig into in her own way (which, honestly, most of us would do).What follows is an obsessive search through unreliable sources and dark references, especially tied to the songs in the tapes, sung by a couple in their sleep.A film built around one of our everyday companions, like a podcast, could have offered a sharper commentary on our obsession with consuming trauma as entertainment...like voices from a TV left on in the background...never disturbing enough to make you turn it off, or truly listen.Instead, it never evolves beyond a narrative device-something that only exists when Evy's headphones are on.What remains is a film where very little happens (intentionally so) but without the psychological or emotional depth needed to make that stillness truly claustrophobic and unsettling.Despite the promise of its premise, the film leans heavily on its technical strengths. The sound design does most of the work, crafting an eerie, persistent tension, while the cinematography makes effective use of its confined setting-the house and its rooms.Tuason's direction shows a clear understanding of space and restraint-a filmmaker with potential and ideas, though still in an in utero stage.Unfortunately, even well-developed details can turn against you when the script feels like it gives in to its own ghosts.My experience with "Undertone" (and my expectations were quite high) was defined more by boredom than dread. I kept waiting for the film to either say something meaningful or push its premise into more disturbing territory, but it never quite commits-becoming, at times, predictable.There is certainly an audience for this. Fans of ambient, sensory-driven horror-films like "Skinamarink," "Berberian Sound Studio," or "Pontypool"-may find "Undertone" immersive, even emotionally evocative.But for me, it felt more like a melody of old, familiar ideas sold as something intriguing-restrained in its pursuit of innovation, and ultimately hollow.

Balls Up is a film that quickly proves itself to be more than just weak-it's outright pointless. The story lacks any real substance, drifting aimlessly without direction or purpose. Instead of clever humor, it relies on crude, low-level jokes that feel cheap and unnecessary. The tone comes across as vulgar rather than entertaining, making it difficult to enjoy or even tolerate at times. The performances don't help either, with acting that feels exaggerated, awkward, and often unintentionally silly. Overall, Balls Up is a shallow and disappointing film that offers no real value and is simply a waste of time.

Walking into Dhurandhar 2 with high expectations and long-built anticipation, I got to experience it exactly the way I had hoped to, and once again, keeping all socio-politics and the obvious, expected talks of propaganda aside, purely from a cinematic storytelling perspective, it delivers, and how.It starts off with the origin tale, spending enough time to keep you invested in the story, before seamlessly transitioning back to the political conflicts of the first part.The use of classic music and sound design remains on point yet again, especially during the high-octane, gory action. Captured beautifully, it delivers just the right amount of mass needed. Beyond the performances, this is what truly stands out as the film's biggest strength.Needless to say, Ranveer Singh is a beast and the epitome of the ideal massy hero in this one, but he delivers equally well in the emotional moments too. A special mention to Arjun Rampal for carrying out his menacing side so effortlessly.The screenplay feels well-balanced, giving each character their moments to shine, with sprinkles of comedic writing that further balance its otherwise bloody tone.With multiple fast-paced twists and montages in the second half, the narrative takes an interesting turn, building up to a slightly long yet gripping climax, with a few cliché one-liners often heard in films following the patriotic template.For someone who walked in with expectations, I would say it has several moments where it exceeds itself and goes beyond them.Those who walk in with a clear, open mind are sure to be met with plenty of surprises, keeping them on the edge of their seats until the very end.All in all, given the hype and excitement surrounding it since the first part, one can easily bet that this is a film with the potential to enjoy a long theatrical run and even break records. So if you are interested, do yourself a favour and watch it on the big screen.It is sure to elevate the experience in ways one cannot imagine.

titulo: Ready or Not 2: Here I Come
sinopse: This is the rare follow-up that manages to capture the lightning-in-a-bottle energy of the original while carving out its own chaotic identity. Despite some frustrating "horror movie logic" during the action beats, the film succeeds because it understands its own strengths: dark humor, ritualistic tension, and a cast that is fully committed to the madness. It doesn't try to reinvent the wheel or preach a message; it simply doubles down on being a high-stakes, creative survival game that keeps you guessing until the very end.
